theory.intro: Two rectangular prisms can have the same volume even though their lengths, widths and heights are different. What matters is that the product length $\times$ width $\times$ height is the same.
facts: Different dimensions can give the **same product**, so the same volume.
Rearranging the same three dimensions (e.g. $2 \times 3 \times 4$) gives the same volume.
steps: Work out each prism's volume by multiplying its three dimensions.
Compare the products to decide if the volumes are equal.
To match a volume, find three numbers whose product equals it.

foundation.q: Find the volume of a box $2$ cm by $3$ cm by $4$ cm.
Find the volume of a box $1$ cm by $4$ cm by $6$ cm.
Do boxes $2 \times 3 \times 4$ and $1 \times 4 \times 6$ have the same volume?
Find the volume of a box $2$ cm by $2$ cm by $3$ cm.
Find another box with volume $12\text{ cm}^{3}$ using whole-number sides.
Find the volume of a box $2$ cm by $4$ cm by $5$ cm.
Do boxes $1 \times 5 \times 8$ and $2 \times 4 \times 5$ have the same volume?
Find the volume of a box $3$ cm by $3$ cm by $2$ cm.
Find another box with volume $18\text{ cm}^{3}$ using whole-number sides.
Boxes $2 \times 3 \times 5$ and $1 \times 5 \times 6$: do they have equal volume?

development.q: A storage box is $4 \times 5 \times 6$. Find another box with the same volume.
Do prisms $3 \times 4 \times 5$ and $2 \times 5 \times 6$ have the same volume?
A fish tank holds $48\text{ cm}^{3}$. List two different sets of whole-number dimensions.
A box is $6 \times 4 \times 2$. A second box has the same height $2$ and volume. Give its base.
Two shipping crates have volume $72\text{ m}^{3}$. One is $3 \times 4 \times 6$. Give different dimensions for the other.
Do boxes $2 \times 6 \times 5$ and $3 \times 4 \times 5$ have the same volume?
A prism is $8 \times 3 \times 2$. Give another prism with the same volume but different dimensions.
A room is $5 \times 4 \times 3$ m. A second room has the same floor area but height $5$ m. Find its volume and floor area.
Find three different boxes (whole-number sides) each with volume $36\text{ cm}^{3}$.
Which has the larger volume: a box $4 \times 5 \times 3$ or a box $2 \times 6 \times 5$?

mastery.q: A box of volume $64\text{ cm}^{3}$ is a cube. Give a different (non-cube) box with the same volume.
Two fish tanks hold the same volume. One is $30 \times 20 \times 40$ cm. The other is $40$ cm wide and $30$ cm high. Find its length.
How many different boxes with whole-number sides have a volume of exactly $8\text{ cm}^{3}$? List them.
A shipping container is $12 \times 2 \times 2$ m. A new design keeps the same volume but is $4$ m wide and $2$ m high. Find its length.
Prism A is $5 \times 4 \times 3$ and prism B is $2 \times 6 \times 5$. Do they have equal volume?
A box of volume $100\text{ cm}^{3}$ must have a height of $4$ cm. Give possible whole-number length and width.
A storage box $3 \times 5 \times 4$ is repacked into a box of the same volume with height $2$ cm. Find a possible base.
Box P is $2 \times 5 \times 6$ and box Q is $3 \times 4 \times 5$. Which holds more, and by how much?
mastery.a: Box P by $0$ — they are equal ($60\text{ cm}^{3}$)

mastery.q: A cube has volume $27\text{ cm}^{3}$. Give a rectangular prism with the same volume and whole-number sides.
A tank of volume $120\text{ cm}^{3}$ has length $6$ cm and width $5$ cm. A second tank has the same volume, length $4$ cm and width $5$ cm. Compare their heights.
mastery.a: First $4$ cm, second $6$ cm — second is $2$ cm taller
